Let’s now define the conditional flow map as,

ψt(x0∣x1)=σtx0+μt(x1)\psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1) = \sigma_t x_0 + \mu_t(x_1)ψt​(x0​∣x1​)=σt​x0​+μt​(x1​)

If x0∼N(0,I)x_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)x0​∼N(0,I), then ψt(x0∣x1)\psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1)ψt​(x0​∣x1​) is the canonical transformation of the flow,

ψt(x0∣x1)=σtx0+μt(x1)\psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1) = \sigma_t x_0 + \mu_t(x_1)ψt​(x0​∣x1​)=σt​x0​+μt​(x1​)

This is the simplest form of interpolation of two distributions, only allowing shifts and scaling.

If we define σt=1−t\sigma_t = 1 - tσt​=1−t and μt(x1)=tx1\mu_t(x_1) = t x_1μt​(x1​)=tx1​, then we can interpolate between two distributions,

ψt(x0∣x1)=(1−t)x0+tx1.\psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1) = (1 - t) x_0 + t x_1.ψt​(x0​∣x1​)=(1−t)x0​+tx1​.

Thus, we can rewrite our objective as,

Ext∼pt(xt∣x1),x1∼q(x1)[∥vt(xt)−ddtψt(x0∣x1)∥2]\mathbb{E}_{x_t \sim p_t(x_t | x_1), x_1 \sim q(x_1)} \left[ \Vert v_t(x_t) - \frac{d}{dt} \psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1) \Vert^2 \right]Ext​∼pt​(xt​∣x1​),x1​∼q(x1​)​[∥vt​(xt​)−dtd​ψt​(x0​∣x1​)∥2]

We can reparameterize this as,

Ext∼pt(xt∣x1),x1∼q(x1)[∥vt(ψt(x0∣x1))−ddtψt(x0∣x1)∥2]\mathbb{E}_{x_t \sim p_t(x_t | x_1), x_1 \sim q(x_1)} \left[ \Vert v_t(\psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1)) - \frac{d}{dt} \psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1) \Vert^2 \right]Ext​∼pt​(xt​∣x1​),x1​∼q(x1​)​[∥vt​(ψt​(x0​∣x1​))−dtd​ψt​(x0​∣x1​)∥2]

The derivative of ψt(x0∣x1)\psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1)ψt​(x0​∣x1​) is,

ddtψt(x0∣x1)=x1−x0\frac{d}{dt} \psi_t(x_0 \mid x_1) = x_1 - x_0dtd​ψt​(x0​∣x1​)=x1​−x0​

So at training time we,

  1. Sample noise from a noise distribution — x0∼N(0,I)x_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)x0​∼N(0,I).
  2. Sample a data point from our dataset — x1∼Dx_1 \sim \mathcal{D}x1​∼D.
  3. Sample a t∼U(0,1)t \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1)t∼U(0,1).
  4. Then we calculate xtx_txt​ by linearly interpolating between x0x_0x0​ and x1x_1x1​ — xt=(1−t)x0+tx1x_t = (1 - t) x_0 + t x_1xt​=(1−t)x0​+tx1​.
  5. (4) is our input to our neural network vtv_tvt​.
  6. The network tries to predict the vector from x0x_0x0​ to x1x_1x1​.

One Coin, Three Views

At this point, the big picture is hopefully clearer, denoising diffusion, score matching, and flow matching are not three unrelated ideas. They are three ways of describing the same transport problem.

Doob’s hhh-transform gives the reverse drift

One of the nicest conceptual connections from the course is that the reverse diffusion drift has the same structure as Doob’s hhh-transform 8The hhh-transform is the classical way to turn an unconditional diffusion into a bridge conditioned on future information.. If we condition a forward process to end up in the right place, we add a drift correction of the form L2∇log⁡hL^2 \nabla \log hL2∇logh. That is exactly the same kind of correction term that appears in the reverse SDE, where the score ∇log⁡pt\nabla \log p_t∇logpt​ pushes noisy samples back toward regions of high data density.

So the reverse diffusion story is not some separate trick. It is a continuous-time bridge, we start from a simple process, then bend it toward the desired endpoint distribution.

Gaussian bridges tie score matching and flow matching together

Now take the Gaussian probability path

xt=αtx1+σtϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I).x_t = \alpha_t x_1 + \sigma_t \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I).xt​=αt​x1​+σt​ϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I).

This one bridge already contains the three viewpoints. The diffusion view asks for an SDE whose marginals follow this path. The score matching view learns

st(xt)=∇xtlog⁡pt(xt),s_t(x_t) = \nabla_{x_t} \log p_t(x_t),st​(xt​)=∇xt​​logpt​(xt​),

the field that tells us how to move toward higher density. The flow matching view learns the velocity field that transports samples along the same path.

The key identity is the Gaussian score identity, sometimes presented as a Fisher or Tweedie-style relation:

st(xt)=E[−xt−αtx1σt2∣xt]=−1σtE[ϵ∣xt].s_t(x_t) = \mathbb{E}\left[-\frac{x_t - \alpha_t x_1}{\sigma_t^2} \mid x_t\right] = -\frac{1}{\sigma_t} \mathbb{E}[\epsilon \mid x_t].st​(xt​)=E[−σt2​xt​−αt​x1​​∣xt​]=−σt​1​E[ϵ∣xt​].

So the score, the predicted noise, and the denoiser all carry the same posterior information. Equivalently,

E[x1∣xt]=xt+σt2st(xt)αt.\mathbb{E}[x_1 \mid x_t] = \frac{x_t + \sigma_t^2 s_t(x_t)}{\alpha_t}.E[x1​∣xt​]=αt​xt​+σt2​st​(xt​)​.

And the marginal flow-matching vector field is just a reparameterization of that same score field:

ut(xt)=(α˙t−σ˙tσtαt)E[x1∣xt]+σ˙tσtxt=α˙tαtxt+σt2(α˙tαt−σ˙tσt)st(xt).u_t(x_t) = \left(\dot{\alpha}_t - \frac{\dot{\sigma}_t}{\sigma_t} \alpha_t\right)\mathbb{E}[x_1 \mid x_t] + \frac{\dot{\sigma}_t}{\sigma_t} x_t = \frac{\dot{\alpha}_t}{\alpha_t} x_t + \sigma_t^2 \left(\frac{\dot{\alpha}_t}{\alpha_t} - \frac{\dot{\sigma}_t}{\sigma_t}\right) s_t(x_t).ut​(xt​)=(α˙t​−σt​σ˙t​​αt​)E[x1​∣xt​]+σt​σ˙t​​xt​=αt​α˙t​​xt​+σt2​(αt​α˙t​​−σt​σ˙t​​)st​(xt​).

This is the cleanest sense in which they are the same coin,

  1. Fix a probability path from noise to data.
  2. Learn its score, its denoiser, or its velocity field.
  3. Convert between these parameterizations with simple identities.

At the sampler level, the same unification shows up again, DDIM is diffusion with the stochasticity turned down, which is exactly why its first-order update can line up with a flow-matching Euler step after a change of parameterization.

Diffusion keeps the stochasticity. Flow matching sets it to zero. Score matching learns the field that lets you move between the two descriptions.

Footnotes

  1. Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015, Deep Unsupervised Learning using Nonequilibrium Thermodynamics ↩

  2. Anderson, 1982, Reverse-Time Diffusion Equation Models ↩ ↩2 ↩3

  3. Lipman et al., 2022, Flow Matching for Generative Modeling ↩
